EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moving through the Lower Mississippi River Valley developed a well defined squall line that moved across southeast Louisiana and adjacent coastal waters during the afternoon hours of the 13th. Numerous reports of wind damage occurred across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported uprooted on Tartan Drive in the Bissonet Subdivision of Metairie. The event time was estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moving through the Lower Mississippi River Valley developed a well defined squall line that moved across southeast Louisiana and adjacent coastal waters during the afternoon hours of the 13th. Numerous reports of wind damage occurred across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds across US Highway 190 at Jackson Avenue. Power was reported out again in Mandeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moving through the Lower Mississippi River Valley developed a well defined squall line that moved across southeast Louisiana and adjacent coastal waters during the afternoon hours of the 13th. Numerous reports of wind damage occurred across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported 8 to 10 inches deep on Gause Boulevard at the entrance to the Huntwyck Subdivision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved quickly into the Central and Southern Plains providing the necessary lift for scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across Northwest Texas and Western Oklahoma during the evening hours of Sunday, Oct. 12th. At the surface, a warm front was rapidly moving northward across East Texas and North Louisiana with low level moisture returning quickly in the wake of the northward moving warm front. A cold front in association with the upper level trough was rapidly moving eastward through Western Oklahoma and Texas. Overnight across Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, Northeast Texas and Northwest Louisiana, moderate instability was in place with increasing low and mid-level shear as the upper trough approached from the west. Storms eventually erupted ahead of the cold front across Central Oklahoma and Northern Texas, maturing into a squall line as it approached the region during the predawn hours of Monday, Oct. 13th. As the squall line traversed through the region, widespread wind damage was reported associated with straight line winds. Several short track, intense tornadoes developed along the leading edge of the squall line as well across portions of Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas and Northern Louisiana. The storms finally exited the region during the afternoon hours of Oct. 13th. Post-event Storm Surveys determined that 6 tornadoes occurred with this severe weather outbreak, one of which produced 1 fatality and 4 injuries in Little River County, Arkansas. Another tornado produced widespread damage across Ouachita Parish in Monroe, Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed near Bawcomville, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very moist and unstable air surged northward over southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi ahead of a cold front moving through the Great Plains. Thunderstorms developed in this moist airmass, producing very heavy rainfall, which caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local news media showed live footage of multiple high water rescues across the city of New Orleans.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass allowed the development of isolated thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana during the afternoon of July 2. Several reports of wind damage were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage occurred to the roof and one wall of a business in Laplace.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass allowed the development of isolated thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana during the afternoon of July 2. Several reports of wind damage were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported blown down in and just north of Greensburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dying thunderstorm complex over Arkansas on the morning of the 8th provided the push for development of thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana during the afternoon hours. Lightning from the storms triggered two house fires in the Baton Rouge area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning striking a roof triggered a fire that produced significant damage to a home on Kenmore Avenue in Baton Rouge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dying thunderstorm complex over Arkansas on the morning of the 8th provided the push for development of thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana during the afternoon hours. Lightning from the storms triggered two house fires in the Baton Rouge area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning striking a roof triggered a fire that produced significant damage to a home on Destrehan Drive in Baton Rouge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the mid and late afternoon hours on Monday, August 11th, near and south of a weak surface boundary that was reinforced south from storms that developed the previous day. This large boundary extended along the Interstate 20 corridor of North Central and East Texas into North Louisiana, with strong daytime heating atop a very moist air mass contributing to moderate instability, resulting in shower and thunderstorm development. These storms produced numerous outflow boundaries across North Louisiana, which allowed for these storms to become more widespread and form a small mesoscale convective system, as they accelerated south across North Central Louisiana. Damaging winds were noted at times, resulting in downed trees and power lines over the area. A second cluster of showers and thunderstorms developed over East Central Texas south of the stationary outflow boundary, and quickly spread east southeast across East Texas south of Interstate 20. Some of these storms also became severe over Nacogdoches, Shelby, and Sabine counties, where downbursts downed numerous trees over these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large limbs and powerlines were downed on North Second and Cotton Streets in Jena, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active sea breeze boundary moved northward from Central Louisiana, producing showers and thunderstorms across portions of North Central and East Central Louisiana during the afternoon hours of August 17th.  The boundary provided the lift necessary for these thunderstorms to form and they produced some wind damage before dissipating later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines were blown down on 5th Street.  Powerlines were also reported downed on Hwy. 158 in Colfax.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level trough moved out of the Southern Plains and into the Lower Mississippi Valley during the late afternoon and evening hours of October 2nd. This upper level trough took on a negative tilt as it moved towards the Four State Region, with vigorous lift resulting in the development of severe thunderstorms in a moderately unstable environment. Strong unidirectional deep layer shear resulted in the convection eventually taking on the shape of a squall line which raced through the region producing widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines were downed on Vivian Airport Road in Vivian, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed shortly after daybreak on Saturday, August 9th across eastern McCurtain County Oklahoma and the northern sections of Southwest Arkansas, along a stationary outflow boundary from earlier storms that developed the previous day over central and eastern Oklahoma, as well as western Arkansas. Additional redevelopment occurred through much of the morning, with these storms moving repeatedly over the same areas. Widespread 2 to 4 inch rainfall totals, with isolated amounts exceeding 5 inches, fell over much of Sevier, Howard, and northern Hempstead counties within a matter of a few hours, resulting in numerous reports of flash flooding throughout Sevier County.||After these storms diminished during the early afternoon, other scattered showers and thunderstorms developed farther south along various outflow boundaries during peak heating and instability. One of these storms became severe, which produced a localized downburst over Claiborne Parish in North Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines were downed north of Lisbon, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms formed along several outflow boundaries and became severe over parts of Northwest and North Central Louisiana during the heat of the afternoon and along the remnants of an earlier outflow boundary from Saturday.  Damage occurred across parts of Bienville Parish (Gibsland) with trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed in and around the Gibsland community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow aloft was present across the Southern Plains and Lower Mississippi valley during this period. A disturbance moved southward out of this flow into the Four State Region. Strong mid level lapse rates and elevated instability resulted in the development of strong to severe thunderstorms during the predawn hours of October 6th. The storms eventually developed into a Mesoscale Convective Complex producing large hail and widespread wind damage across portions of Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas as well as Northeast Texas and Northwest Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and tropical moisture combined over Southwest Louisiana to produce heavy rain and flooding. This heavy rainfall event capped the one of the wettest summers on record at the Lake Charles Regional Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very moist air mass allowed for very high rainfall rates across Calcasieu Parish during the morning of the 30th. Rainfall amounts ranged from 4 to near 8 inches with the majority falling from sunrise to noon. The Lake Charles Regional Airport received 7.87 inches during the event with around 3 inches falling in under an hour. After 8 AM a call was received from an off-duty NWS meteorologist of a car submerged in a ditch along Interstate 210 near Nelson Road. Some cars were unable to pass the high waters along Nelson Road and many other streets in the city. Portions of Interstates 10 and 210 were temporarily closed due to high water. 1 home was flooded near the Nelson Road and Gauthier Road intersection and a apartment complex also flooded along Nelson Road. Multiple other homes were flooded across the city of Lake Charles. 5 homes were flooded along Dogwood Ave and a few homes also flooded in the Hunters Grove Subdivision in Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and tropical moisture combined over Southwest Louisiana to produce heavy rain and flooding. This heavy rainfall event capped the one of the wettest summers on record at the Lake Charles Regional Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain stretched from south of Lake Charles to near Oakdale with the heaviest falling from Kinder to KLCH. Flooding in Kinder closed multiple streets around the town during the morning into the early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed during the late morning through the afternoon hours on September 2nd across extreme Eastern Texas, North Louisiana, and Southern Arkansas. A couple of these storms became severe during the afternoon, with an EF-1 tornado touching down northeast of Mansfield in Desoto Parish. This tornado snapped numerous trees just east of Clear Lake, where it crossed over the International Paper Plant, peeling back portions of the metal walls and roof. One car was flipped over, and numerous car windows were blown out as well at the IP Plant, as it tracked east into western Red River Parish. Several other trees were snapped across mainly open pasture, before the tornado crossed over Highway 1 and lifted prior to reaching the Red River. This parent thunderstorm re-intensified as it shifted northeast into southern Bienville Parish, where a large swath of 60+ mph winds blew across the western and central portions of the parish. This resulted in numerous trees being downed across the parish before weakening shortly there afterwards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in and around the community of Ringgold, Louisiana.

A warm front was situated west to east across Northeast Texas, extreme Northern Louisiana and Southwest Arkansas during the late afternoon hours of October 12th.  This warm front was moving northward ahead of increasing southwesterly flow aloft in advance of a strong upper level trough that began to effect the region on October 13th.  The warm sector south of the returning warm front the afternoon and evening of the 12th became moderately unstable and was uncapped.  Widely scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed during this time period which resulted in strong and damaging wind gusts along with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported to be downed just northwest of Many, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted near the NWS Lake Charles office.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Calcasieu Parish 911 call center reported a funnel cloud around one mile southeast of KLCH. The funnel was also visible from the NWS office.

Some of these storms also became severe over Nacogdoches, Shelby, and Sabine counties, where downbursts downed numerous trees over these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Hwy. 126 west of Dodson, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was situated west to east across Northeast Texas, extreme Northern Louisiana and Southwest Arkansas during the late afternoon hours of October 12th.  This warm front was moving northward ahead of increasing southwesterly flow aloft in advance of a strong upper level trough that began to effect the region on October 13th.  The warm sector south of the returning warm front the afternoon and evening of the 12th became moderately unstable and was uncapped.  Widely scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed during this time period which resulted in strong and damaging wind gusts along with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed west of Blanchard, Louisiana.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey team determined that damage south of Mansfield, Louisiana was consistent with that of an EF-1 tornado.  This tornado touched down approximately 4.5 miles south of Mansfield just east of Hwy. 171.  The tornado moved east along a near one mile track and lifted at the intersection of La. 513 and Oxford Cutoff Road.  Damage consisted of uprooted and snapped trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along an outflow boundary across Union, northern Ouachita, and Morehouse Parishes during the late afternoon and early evening of September 6th, and slowly drifted south across much of Ouachita Parish, including the city of Monroe. One particular storm was locally strong, producing estimated 40 mph winds over the city, which resulted in a metal roof peeling back off of a lumber warehouse on Desiard Street, landing on a few cars in the parking lot. From a long-time resident and active storm spotter in Monroe, these buildings are old and poorly constructed, which likely contributed to the damage from these wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal roof was blown off the Russell Moore Lumber Warehouse on Desiard Street in Monrow, landing on several cars in the parking lot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early afternoon hours of July 23rd, a complex of showers and thunderstorms moved southeast into Eastern Oklahoma and Northwest Arkansas, after originally developing early that morning across portions of Nebraska and central Kansas. This complex encountered a very unstable air mass near a weak surface trough over Northern and Eastern Oklahoma and Northern Arkansas as it continued southeastward. At the same time, other scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over Central and Eastern Arkansas near this surface trough, while also aided by a weak upper level disturbance sinking south across Arkansas and the Mid-Mississippi Valley. The Eastern Oklahoma storm complex absorbed the growing storm complex over Central Arkansas, resulting in a large mesoscale convective system (MCS) intensifying and surging south into Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas during the mid and late afternoon hours. Widespread wind damage was reported across much of Central and Southern Arkansas as well as Southeast Oklahoma, with a large cold pool of air surging south into Northeast Texas and North Louisiana by late afternoon and early evening. Wind gusts ranged from 50-70 mph with this MCS as it surged south across much of the Ark-La-Tex, resulting in numerous downed trees, power lines, and structural damage. This storm complex weakened during the early evening hours, although the resultant cold pool still produced 40-45 mph wind gusts which produced minor tree damage over portions of Deep East Texas that did not see showers or thunderstorms. Over 500,000 AEP SWEPCO customers were without power after the storms blew through, with many thousands of other customers with other utility companies also without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large 80ft oak tree fell on top of a house at Kingston Road and Melba Street in south Shreveport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed during the late morning through the afternoon hours on September 2nd across extreme Eastern Texas, North Louisiana, and Southern Arkansas. A couple of these storms became severe during the afternoon, with an EF-1 tornado touching down northeast of Mansfield in Desoto Parish. This tornado snapped numerous trees just east of Clear Lake, where it crossed over the International Paper Plant, peeling back portions of the metal walls and roof. One car was flipped over, and numerous car windows were blown out as well at the IP Plant, as it tracked east into western Red River Parish. Several other trees were snapped across mainly open pasture, before the tornado crossed over Highway 1 and lifted prior to reaching the Red River. This parent thunderstorm re-intensified as it shifted northeast into southern Bienville Parish, where a large swath of 60+ mph winds blew across the western and central portions of the parish. This resulted in numerous trees being downed across the parish before weakening shortly there afterwards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down on La Coupe Road where it peeled back the metal roof of a barn.  This tornado also snapped numerous large limbs, some of which landed on a home before continuing east across the International Paper Plant.  Portions of the metal walls and roof at the IP Plant were peeled back with several trees snapped.  One car was flipped over and numerous car windows were blown out. The tornado continued east across sample road before entering western Red River Parish.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey crew determined than an EF-1 tornado touched down in Ouachita Parish. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level trough moved out of the Southern Plains and into the Lower Mississippi Valley during the late afternoon and evening hours of October 2nd. This upper level trough took on a negative tilt as it moved towards the Four State Region, with vigorous lift resulting in the development of severe thunderstorms in a moderately unstable environment. Strong unidirectional deep layer shear resulted in the convection eventually taking on the shape of a squall line which raced through the region producing widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and powerlines were downed south of Haughton, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Dec 23, just enough ingredients came together to support numerous severe storms ahead of a cold front. Across the Lower Mississippi River Valley, peak heating contributed to decent instability in the developing warm sector in advance of the front. Sufficient low level wind shear and strong winds aloft were also in place as a decent upper low was located to our north. This helped to support organized thunderstorm activity along with quite a few supercell storms.||A supercell thunderstorm developed in northeast Louisiana and split into different thunderstorms. What we call a left-mover began to quickly move to the north. This storm has a rotating updraft that rotates anticyclonically (clockwise) . Due to large hail being generated aloft in the storm, a three-body scatter spike (TBSS) or hail spike developed on radar, indicating very large hail was possible. This storm had produced golf ball to grapefruit sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of hail, ranging from quarter to golfball size, fell along the eastern side of the parish. Hen egg sized hail fell along Louisiana Highway 562.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Less than a week after a snow and freezing rain event across the region and only 3 days after the ice had melted, cold air crept back in while another upper level disturbance traversed the area. Area temperatures were slightly colder during this event with precipitation falling as mostly sleet and snow; however freezing rain occurred as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The event started with light freezing rain in the early morning across Rapides Parish before changing over to snow. Around one tenth of an inch of ice accumulated across Alexandria with 1.5 inches officially recorded for the city. Snow totals across the parish ranged from around 1.5 to just under 3 inches. Interstate 49 was closed during the event along with most bridges. Rapides Parish Police Department responded to 23 weather related traffic incedents.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The event started with light freezing rain in the early morning across Avoyelles Parish before changing over to snow. Around one tenth of an inch of ice accumulated across the area with snow accumulations ranging from around an inch and a half to just over 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept through during the 5th with strong north winds behind the boundary pushing tides below normal and hindering ship traffic. Very cold temperatures also swept into South Louisiana behind the boundary. Two people were reported dead from exposure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A homeless woman in Opelousas was found dead after spending a cold night outside. The low temperature at KLFT, which is 23 miles south of Opelousas, during the morning of the 7th was 20 degrees. The woman refused to go to a shelter the previous evening and was found by a firefighter the next morning behind a fire station.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The event started with light freezing rain and sleet in the early morning across Vernon Parish before changing over to snow. Under one tenth of an inch of ice accumulated across the area with snow accumulations ranging from 1 to 2 inches.

Under one tenth of an inch of ice accumulated across the area with snow accumulations ranging from under half an inch to one and a half inch.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The event started with light freezing rain and sleet in the early morning across Allen Parish before changing over to snow. Under one tenth of an inch of ice accumulated across the area with snow accumulations ranging from half an inch to just under 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Less than a week after a snow and freezing rain event across the region and only 3 days after the ice had melted, cold air crept back in while another upper level disturbance traversed the area. Under one tenth of an inch of ice accumulated across the area with sleet accumulations ranging from around one tenth of an inch to three quarters of an inch. Most bridges and Interstate 10 remained open during the event, however Interstate 210 closed as all the overpasses and the Prien Lake-Calcasieu River Bridge were not treated and froze up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bitterly cold airmass descended on southern Mississippi and southeast Louisiana during the night of the 26th and during the day on the 27th. A strong upper level jet disturbance moved across the area on the 28th, producing a large area of sleet and freezing rain. Temperatures remained at or below the freezing mark during much or all of the precipitation event on the 28th, even south of Lake Pontchartrain in the New Orleans metropolitan area. Travel was significantly impacted on bridges, overpasses and other elevated roadways. Portions of every Interstate in southeast Louisiana, plus the Lake Pontchartrain Causeway were closed at times on the 28th into the morning of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet accumulations were near one half inch. One quarter inch of ice was covering all roads in Autumn Run and Collins Place subdivisions. All precipitation was freezing on contact. Ice glazed trees and power lines along Louisiana Route 447. One half inch accumulation was also reported in Denham Springs by a CoCoRAHS observer. A Livingston Parish resident reported three quarters of an inch of sleet and freezing rain accumulation 10 miles north of Walker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through the region during the 2nd with strong north winds behind the boundary. These strong winds pushed tides below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through during the 2nd with north winds driving the tide levels below normal. The tide gage at Cameron reported water levels falling below one foot below mllw during the morning and into the afternoon. The tide fell to -2.39 feet at 9:56 AM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass plunged south to the gulf coast while an upper level disturbance moved across the region. Rain, snow, and sleet moved into the region during the late afternoon of the 23rd and gradually turned to all snow across Central Louisiana. Farther south along highway 190 and Interstate 10 sleet and freezing was the dominate precipitation. At the coast mostly rain and freezing rain occurred. Area bridges and overpasses iced over and most were closed. Around 200 traffic accidents occurred during the event with 2 becoming fatal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow flurries and light rain began during the late afternoon of the 23rd but changed over to freezing rain during the evening. Sleet and light freezing rain continued through noon the next day with freezing drizzle remaining into the afternoon. Ice accumulation reached around one quarter of an inch early in the morning of the 24th with sporadic power outages reported by 4AM. By sunrise most of lower Cameron Parish was without power. Ice accumulation reached between 0.1 to near 0.4 across the eastern sections of Cameron Parish with the highest totals in the northern sections.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold airmass moved down through the north central Plains to the mid Mississippi Valley into the ArklaMiss region. A deep upper trough and cold temperatures aloft persisted to the east of the Mississippi River for the past few weeks with another shot of arctic air that began to move into the region the afternoon of January 23rd. A weak upper disturbance increased moisture across the area, and enough moisture and cold air was available for snow to begin falling on the afternoon of January 23rd. The highest snowfall totals of 1-3 inches occurred in northern Concordia to southern Catahoula parishes in Louisiana. Snowfall persisted into the early afternoon hours of January 24th before the moisture moved out the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snow fell across the southern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated yet elevated strong thunderstorms developed during the evening hours across Northwest Louisiana. These storms developed along the tail end of a departing shortwave. Flow aloft was weakly divergent with weak instability closer to the surface.

Strong low pressure developed over southern Texas and moved toward northern Mississippi during the daytime hours. The associated cold front moved through an unstable airmass, triggering scattered thunderstorms across southern Mississippi and southeast Louisiana. A few of the storms were severe with at least one tornado reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The survey team found mostly EF1 tornado damage and found a few locations with low end EF2 damage. Damage began south of Louisiana Highway 16 near Highway 1054. Near this location, numerous trees were laid down or snapped off with some oriented to the northwest and some to the southeast. A few homes also sustained significant roof damage. On Leander Lane, 3 or 4 homes had varying roof damage with one well built home with more than half its roof pulled off and a wall collapsed in. An unanchored 12x24 shed was slid off its foundation and another outbuilding was destroyed. Two travel trailers were moved 50 yards and destroyed. Farther northeast on Eastwood Road, pine trees were snapped. Trees were also snapped near Katherine Holton Road and Chappepeela Ridge Road, along with a well built home having part of its second story roof pulled off and a wall collapsed. The tornado track ended shortly after this location near Rolling Pine Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a very cold atmosphere already in place, an upper level storm system moved out of the Texas Hill Country and into Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas and Northwest Louisiana during the afternoon hours of February 11th.  Precipitation became widespread across the region late in the afternoon and especially during the overnight hours of February 11th and the 12th.  During the onset of precipitation, a mixture of rain and sleet was the predominant precipitation type with some sleet accumulations exceeding one inch across portions of Northwest Louisiana.  There was even a brief transition of moderate snow across portions of the region as well. During the evening and overnight hours of the event, the transition turned to predominantly freezing rain with some hefty accumulations noted across the region.  Ice accumulations of one quarter of an inch were common across much of Northwest Louisiana with totals of one half inch common across some parishes in Northeast Louisiana.  These higher ice accumulation totals were noted across Union, Lincoln, Ouachita, Jackson and Caldwell Parishes.  Impacts included numerous automobile accidents, downed trees and powerlines as well as thousands without power across the northern half of the state.

During the afternoon of Dec 23, just enough ingredients came together to support numerous severe storms ahead of a cold front. Across the Lower Mississippi River Valley, peak heating contributed to decent instability in the developing warm sector in advance of the front. Sufficient low level wind shear and strong winds aloft were also in place as a decent upper low was located to our north. This helped to support organized thunderstorm activity along with quite a few supercell storms.||A supercell thunderstorm developed in northeast Louisiana and split into different thunderstorms. What we call a left-mover began to quickly move to the north. This storm has a rotating updraft that rotates anticyclonically (clockwise) . Due to large hail being generated aloft in the storm, a three-body scatter spike (TBSS) or hail spike developed on radar, indicating very large hail was possible. This storm had produced golf ball to grapefruit sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter sized hail fell around Vidalia.

Traffic accidents began during the early morning hours of the 24th with one being fatal as all bridges in the Parish became iced and eventually closed. A large portion of the parish received around 1/4 of an inch of ice accumulation; however some southern sections received more as the transition to sleet and snow never occurred. All schools and public offices were also closed due to icing on area roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass plunged south to the gulf coast while an upper level disturbance moved across the region. Rain, snow, and sleet moved into the region during the late afternoon of the 23rd and gradually turned to all snow across Central Louisiana. Farther south along highway 190 and Interstate 10 sleet and freezing was the dominate precipitation. At the coast mostly rain and freezing rain occurred. Area bridges and overpasses iced over and most were closed. Around 200 traffic accidents occurred during the event with 2 becoming fatal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet moved into Rapides Parish during the evening of the 23rd, however precipitation soon changed to snow. Snow kept falling through the night and into the morning of the 24th. Snow accumulation ranged from just below 2.5 inches to near 4 inches. All bridges and overpasses along Interstate 49 were closed as well as bridges across the Red River during the event and into the night following the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Dec 23, just enough ingredients came together to support numerous severe storms ahead of a cold front. Across the Lower Mississippi River Valley, peak heating contributed to decent instability in the developing warm sector in advance of the front. Sufficient low level wind shear and strong winds aloft were also in place as a decent upper low was located to our north. This helped to support organized thunderstorm activity along with quite a few supercell storms.||A supercell thunderstorm developed in northeast Louisiana and split into different thunderstorms. What we call a left-mover began to quickly move to the north. This storm has a rotating updraft that rotates anticyclonically (clockwise) . Due to large hail being generated aloft in the storm, a three-body scatter spike (TBSS) or hail spike developed on radar, indicating very large hail was possible. This storm had produced golf ball to grapefruit sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of mainly golfball to baseball sized hail occurred from just east of Wildsville to just west of Clayton. A vehicle was damaged by some of the golfball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a very cold atmosphere already in place, an upper level storm system moved out of the Texas Hill Country and into Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas and Northwest Louisiana during the afternoon hours of February 11th.  Precipitation became widespread across the region late in the afternoon and especially during the overnight hours of February 11th and the 12th.  During the onset of precipitation, a mixture of rain and sleet was the predominant precipitation type with some sleet accumulations exceeding one inch across portions of Northwest Louisiana.  There was even a brief transition of moderate snow across portions of the region as well. During the evening and overnight hours of the event, the transition turned to predominantly freezing rain with some hefty accumulations noted across the region.  Ice accumulations of one quarter of an inch were common across much of Northwest Louisiana with totals of one half inch common across some parishes in Northeast Louisiana.  These higher ice accumulation totals were noted across Union, Lincoln, Ouachita, Jackson and Caldwell Parishes.  Impacts included numerous automobile accidents, downed trees and powerlines as well as thousands without power across the northern half of the state.

Low pressure moving across central Louisiana and southern Mississippi triggered several thunderstorms across southeast Louisiana. Several reports of weak tornadoes and minor wind damage were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The St. Helena Parish Emergency Manager relayed a report from the local electric utility company of several trees blown down by thunderstorm winds in the vicinity of Highway 1036 and Highway 441 near Montpelier. The event time was estimated based on radar.

A warm and moist air mass was pulled north from the gulf ahead of a cold front. As a weak surface low pushed across the region a strong short wave also moved across the northern gulf coast. This set up provided support for a few severe thunderstorms which mostly produced hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter southeast of Lafayette reported around 70 feet of wooden fence blown down with metal support poles for the fence bent around 20 degrees off the ground. A few shingles were a blown off a near by house as well as a few large branches broken along Presbytere Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across central Louisiana and southern Mississippi triggered several thunderstorms across southeast Louisiana. Several reports of weak tornadoes and minor wind damage were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey found some spotty minor damage southwest of Walker. Shingles were removed from several roofs and a few small trees were snapped. The estimated peak wind speed was 65 to 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across central Louisiana and southern Mississippi triggered several thunderstorms across southeast Louisiana. Several reports of weak tornadoes and minor wind damage were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The meso-cyclone thunderstorm that produced a weak tornado south of Walker produced another weak tornadoes north of Livingston. A NWS damage survey discovered tree damage along Louisiana Highway 1036, including several pine trees that were snapped at the base. Estimated wind speed was 86 to 95 mph with a maximum path width of 300 yards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report with a video of 1.25 inch hail was received from Tulla through Facebook. Another report a couple minutes later reported hail to 2.5 inches in the same town.

Strong low pressure developed over southern Texas and moved toward northern Mississippi during the daytime hours. The associated cold front moved through an unstable airmass, triggering scattered thunderstorms across southern Mississippi and southeast Louisiana. A few of the storms were severe with at least one tornado reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen reported several trees were uprooted to the north of Franklinton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved across the Abbeville area producing hail around one and a half inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers with embedded thunderstorms developed along and just ahead of a cold front over Deep East Texas and North Louisiana, enhanced by a weak surface low/wave that developed over Southeast Texas and shifted northeast across Central Louisiana during the afternoon. One isolated strong thunderstorm produced nickel size hail southwest of Burke near the Trinity/Angelina County, Texas line as well as near the communities of Woden and KingTown Landing southeast of Nacogdoches. Locally gusty winds also downed large tree limbs near Lufkin, TX before the storm later weakened. This same storm did re-intensify slightly and downed a tree near Huxley, TX (Shelby County), and later produced pea to dime size hail in Coushatta, LA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail was reported in Coushatta, Louisiana.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media relayed a report of 1.25 inch hail near Opelousas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few hours of moderate to heavy rain produced minor street flooding in and around the city of Lake Charles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist air mass lifted into South Louisiana during the morning of the 16th ahead of a strong cold front and short wave, however only marginal instability was present as the cold front moved in. This resulted in one isolated severe thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center for Acadia Parish reported trees and power lines down at Morrow Road and Highway 13 north of Crowley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tractor trailer was blown over near the intersection of highway 365 and Highway 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale upper trough was anchored across the Central Plains with subtropical moisture wrapping around the southwest side of the upper low.  This flow helped to enhance showers and thunderstorms across portions of Northwest Louisiana during the morning hours of December 23rd.  This convection developed in advance of an elevated frontal boundary.  Instability was sufficient enough that some of the thunderstorms across Northern Louisiana produced non-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail was reported in Robeline, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass infiltrated the region early on Sunday, January 5th with falling temperatures and strong northwest winds. Temperatures fell through much of the day on January 5th behind the front with winds approaching 30 mph at times, making it feel much colder with very cold wind chills. Skies cleared the evening of the 5th but the strong northwest winds continued with wind chill values near zero to ten degrees above zero witnessed the night of the 5th.  Low temperatures the morning of the 6th and the morning of the 7th were some of the coldest temperatures Northwest Louisiana had seen since February 3 through the 5th of 1996. The extreme cold resulted in many underground water lines freezing and bursting. Below are some low temperatures recorded at various locations for the mornings of the 6th and 7th of January.  Shreveport, Louisiana:  17, 15.  Monroe, Louisiana:  19, 13.

A cold air mass crept back into South and Central Louisiana during the first few days of March. An upper level disturbance moved across the region during the morning of the 4th producing yet another round of freezing rain for the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures slowly fell to freezing just before sunrise during the 4th while light rain fell across the northern portions of the parish. Temperatures remained right at the freezing mark through mid morning while rain was ongoing, however all the ice was melted before noon. Total ice accumulations were very light and no ice related problems were noted in the parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass crept back into South and Central Louisiana during the first few days of March. An upper level disturbance moved across the region during the morning of the 4th producing yet another round of freezing rain for the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began early in the morning across Beauregard Parish with portions of the parish reaching a quarter of an inch accumulation just after sunrise. Most of the parish received around two tenths of an inch accumulation or more. Numerous tree branches were broken and some trees felled during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance moved over a very cold dome of air, producing freezing rain in the Baton Rouge area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain caused icing on Interstate 110, as well as the Mississippi River Bridge on Interstate 10. The icing was responsible for multiple accidents in these areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance moved over a very cold dome of air, producing freezing rain in the Baton Rouge area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light icing was observed on signs and trees in the Walker area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance moved over a very cold dome of air, producing freezing rain in the Baton Rouge area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain with light icing was reported on trees and bridges throughout the parish according to the Sheriff's Office dispatcher.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began early in the morning across the area with portions of the parish reaching to near two tenths of an inch by the end of the event in the afternoon.  A few Mardi Gras events were affected in the parish, however most events went on as planned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass crept back into South and Central Louisiana during the first few days of March. An upper level disturbance moved across the region during the morning of the 4th producing yet another round of freezing rain for the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began early in the morning across the area with portions of the parish reaching to near two tenths of an inch by the end of the event in the afternoon.  A few Mardi Gras events were affected in the parish, however most events went on as planned. Also, some overpasses along Interstate 10 were temporarily closed during the middle of the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass crept back into South and Central Louisiana during the first few days of March. An upper level disturbance moved across the region during the morning of the 4th producing yet another round of freezing rain for the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began early in the morning across the area with portions of the parish reaching to near two tenths of an inch by the end of the event in the afternoon.  A few Mardi Gras events were affected in the parish, however most events went on as planned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moving through the lower Mississippi River Valley aided in the development of several strong to severe thunderstorms. One report of severe weather was received on the morning of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pine trees and large live oak branches were reported downed in the River Oaks Subdivision along Indian Village Road. Event time was estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of upper air disturbances produced strong to severe thunderstorms ahead of a cold front moving into southern Mississippi and southeast Louisiana. The first round of thunderstorms produced several reports of flash flooding, while the second round produced several reports of hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two homes were reported flooded on Rue Charlemagne in east Slidell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of arctic high pressure moved southward into the Southern Plains on Thursday, January 23rd. Light rain began during the morning hours on Thursday as moisture began to override the shallow cold airmass closer to the surface. As the day continued, the depth of the cold air became deeper and deeper with some sleet and snow flurries reported near the Interstate 20 corridor of Northeast Texas and Northern Louisiana. By late afternoon, the change over from light rain to brief periods of sleet and eventually all snow began across the southern half of Northeast Texas into West Central and East Central Louisiana. Snow came down moderately heavy at times reducing visibilities to near one mile, especially across the lower Toledo Bend and Sam Rayburn Country of Deep East Texas into West Central Louisiana. The precipitation ended during the predawn hours Friday, January 24th but not before some impressive snow totals were reported across the region. Below are some snow totals for selected cities across Northwest Louisiana with the snow having begun during the late afternoon or early evening on Thursday, January 23rd and ending during the predawn hours of Friday, January 24th.  Florien: 3 inches, Colfax: 3 inches, Robeline: 2.50 inches, Jena: 2 inches, Cloutierville: 1.50 inches, Coushatta: 1 inch, Many: 1 inch, Winnfield: 1 inch, Natchitoches: 1 inch, Columbia, 0.50 inch.

A strong ridge of arctic high pressure moved southward into the Southern Plains on Thursday, January 23rd. Light rain began during the morning hours on Thursday as moisture began to override the shallow cold airmass closer to the surface. As the day continued, the depth of the cold air became deeper and deeper with some sleet and snow flurries reported near the Interstate 20 corridor of Northeast Texas and Northern Louisiana. By late afternoon, the change over from light rain to brief periods of sleet and eventually all snow began across the southern half of Northeast Texas into West Central and East Central Louisiana. Snow came down moderately heavy at times reducing visibilities to near one mile, especially across the lower Toledo Bend and Sam Rayburn Country of Deep East Texas into West Central Louisiana. The precipitation ended during the predawn hours Friday, January 24th but not before some impressive snow totals were reported across the region. Below are some snow totals for selected cities across Northwest Louisiana with the snow having begun during the late afternoon or early evening on Thursday, January 23rd and ending during the predawn hours of Friday, January 24th.  Florien: 3 inches, Colfax: 3 inches, Robeline: 2.50 inches, Jena: 2 inches, Cloutierville: 1.50 inches, Coushatta: 1 inch, Many: 1 inch, Winnfield: 1 inch, Natchitoches: 1 inch, Columbia, 0.50 inch.

An arctic airmass infiltrated the region early on Sunday, January 5th with falling temperatures and strong northwest winds. Temperatures fell through much of the day on January 5th behind the front with winds approaching 30 mph at times, making it feel much colder with very cold wind chills. Skies cleared the evening of the 5th but the strong northwest winds continued with wind chill values near zero to ten degrees above zero witnessed the night of the 5th.  Low temperatures the morning of the 6th and the morning of the 7th were some of the coldest temperatures Northwest Louisiana had seen since February 3 through the 5th of 1996. The extreme cold resulted in many underground water lines freezing and bursting. Below are some low temperatures recorded at various locations for the mornings of the 6th and 7th of January.  Shreveport, Louisiana:  17, 15.  Monroe, Louisiana:  19, 13.

A cold air mass crept back into South and Central Louisiana during the first few days of March. An upper level disturbance moved across the region during the morning of the 4th producing yet another round of freezing rain for the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began early in the morning across the area with portions of the parish reaching to around a quarter of an inch during the morning. Interstate 10 was temporarily closed between Breaux Bridge and Baton Rouge across the Atchafalaya River until the ice melted in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass crept back into South and Central Louisiana during the first few days of March. An upper level disturbance moved across the region during the morning of the 4th producing yet another round of freezing rain for the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell to freezing during rain around sunrise on the 4th with most of the ice accumulation occurring across the far northern portions of the parish. Ice accumulation of around one tenth of an inch occurred before temperatures rose above freezing around midday to early afternoon. No ice accumulation was reported along the coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough located across New Mexico and the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles slowly moved northeast towards the region on April 6th. Southwest flow dominated the region ahead of the upper level trough which allowed for copious amounts of Pacific and Gulf of Mexico Moisture to ride up and over a stalled frontal boundary along the Southeast Texas and the Southern Louisiana Gulf Coast. Elevated instability and strong lapse rates aloft resulted in the development of large hail initially on the 6th but as the day went on, training of cells resulted in some wind damage with widespread flooding noted across portions of the region as well.

A vigorous upper level trough was directly overhead Northern Louisiana during the afternoon hours of April 8th.  With the aid of daytime heating, low top strong to severe thunderstorms developed across portions of Northwest Louisiana.  Very steep lapse rates resulted in several small hail reports...mainly less than one half inch but the storms also produced strong wind gusts.  One of these gusts resulted in a few downed trees in Lincoln Parish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted northwest of Choudrant, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass crept back into South and Central Louisiana during the first few days of March. An upper level disturbance moved across the region during the morning of the 4th producing yet another round of freezing rain for the region.

The ice was melted during the afternoon when temperatures rose above freezing. Some schools that were attempting to make up time during Mardi Gras for missing days due to ice and snow earlier in the year had to cancel the make-up days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front had slowed its eastward progression during the morning hours of April 14th across portions of North Louisiana.  With the aid of heating...strong to severe thunderstorms developed along the cold front, producing some large hail and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts.  As the day progressed, strong to severe thunderstorms developed in the wake of the front as well due to the presence of an upper level trough approaching from the west. This trough had elevated instability associated with it, along with strong deep layer shear and large mid level lapse rates. The result was the development of scattered strong to severe thunderstorms, some of which produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed near Shiloh just to the southwest of Bryceland, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another arctic cold front moved into the region Monday, January 27th, resulting in very cold and much below normal temperatures across the region. An upper level disturbance moving atop this very cold air mass resulted in areas of snow, sleet and freezing rain across Deep East Texas and the southern sections of North Central Louisiana early Tuesday morning for the second time in less than a week. The wintry mix was more predominant over Deep East Texas, with the snow the more dominant precipitation type across the southern sections of North Central Louisiana. The precipitation ended around mid morning on the 27th, resulting in mainly light snowfall accumulations across the region. Below are some snow totals for selected cities across Northwest Louisiana.  Jena: 4.00 inches, Colfax: 2.50 inches, Florien: 2.00 inches, Dry Prong: 2.00 inches, Natchitoches: 1.50 inches, Winnfield: 1.50 inches, Columbia: 1.50 inches.
Another strong cold front passed through the region during the 14th with tides being driven down during the 15th. Tides fell below one foot below mllw during the early morning of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through during the 14th with north winds driving the tide levels below normal. The tide gage at Freshwater Canal and Lock reported water levels falling below one foot below mllw during the morning of the 15th. The tide fell to -2.22 feet at 7:54 AM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another arctic cold front moved into the region Monday, January 27th, resulting in very cold and much below normal temperatures across the region. An upper level disturbance moving atop this very cold air mass resulted in areas of snow, sleet and freezing rain across Deep East Texas and the southern sections of North Central Louisiana early Tuesday morning for the second time in less than a week. The wintry mix was more predominant over Deep East Texas, with the snow the more dominant precipitation type across the southern sections of North Central Louisiana. The precipitation ended around mid morning on the 27th, resulting in mainly light snowfall accumulations across the region. Below are some snow totals for selected cities across Northwest Louisiana.  Jena: 4.00 inches, Colfax: 2.50 inches, Florien: 2.00 inches, Dry Prong: 2.00 inches, Natchitoches: 1.50 inches, Winnfield: 1.50 inches, Columbia: 1.50 inches.

A deep upper level trough located across New Mexico and the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles slowly moved northeast towards the region on April 6th. Southwest flow dominated the region ahead of the upper level trough which allowed for copious amounts of Pacific and Gulf of Mexico Moisture to ride up and over a stalled frontal boundary along the Southeast Texas and the Southern Louisiana Gulf Coast. Elevated instability and strong lapse rates aloft resulted in the development of large hail initially on the 6th but as the day went on, training of cells resulted in some wind damage with widespread flooding noted across portions of the region as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The underpass at Jackson Street was flooded which caused the closure of the street.

A cold front had slowed its eastward progression during the morning hours of April 14th across portions of North Louisiana.  With the aid of heating...strong to severe thunderstorms developed along the cold front, producing some large hail and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts.  As the day progressed, strong to severe thunderstorms developed in the wake of the front as well due to the presence of an upper level trough approaching from the west. This trough had elevated instability associated with it, along with strong deep layer shear and large mid level lapse rates. The result was the development of scattered strong to severe thunderstorms, some of which produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed in the Grayson community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass infiltrated the region early on Sunday, January 5th with falling temperatures and strong northwest winds. Temperatures fell through much of the day on January 5th behind the front with winds approaching 30 mph at times, making it feel much colder with very cold wind chills. Skies cleared the evening of the 5th but the strong northwest winds continued with wind chill values near zero to ten degrees above zero witnessed the night of the 5th.  Low temperatures the morning of the 6th and the morning of the 7th were some of the coldest temperatures Northwest Louisiana had seen since February 3 through the 5th of 1996. The extreme cold resulted in many underground water lines freezing and bursting. Below are some low temperatures recorded at various locations for the mornings of the 6th and 7th of January.  Shreveport, Louisiana:  17, 15.  Monroe, Louisiana:  19, 13.

Widespread flash flooding took place during the afternoon and evening hours of April 6th.  While the flash flooding receded across the region overnight, ditches were still flooded the following day on April 7th.  A mother and her young son was fishing beside the flooded ditch on April 7th.  The boy slipped and fell into the ditch with a fast current flowing through the ditch at the time.  The boy drowned in the ditch.

An upper level trough took on a negative tilt across Eastern Oklahoma during the afternoon hours of April 24th.  Convergence along an elevated boundary across Southern Arkansas and Northern Louisiana resulted in the development of scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across the region.  The result was mainly hail development with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported near Cross Lake.

A large scale upper trough was anchored across the Central Plains with subtropical moisture wrapping around the southwest side of the upper low.  This flow helped to enhance showers and thunderstorms across portions of Northwest Louisiana during the morning hours of December 23rd.  This convection developed in advance of an elevated frontal boundary.  Instability was sufficient enough that some of the thunderstorms across Northern Louisiana produced non-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail was reported at Hwy. 117 in Provencal, Louisiana.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail was reported in West Monroe, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary pulled up stationary across portions of Southwest Arkansas into Southeast Oklahoma during the daytime hours. A very unstable airmass developed south of the boundary with CAPE values approaching 4000 J/KG and Lifted Indices near -10. While low level shear was weak in the warm sector, deep layer shear was in excess of 50kts and with the help of a strong upper level trough moving out of Central Texas, severe thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon and evening hours of March 28th. Strong lapse rates initially resulted in hail producing thunderstorms, some of which took on the appearance of supercell thunderstorms. Later in the evening, a squall line developed with widespread wind damage noted across the southern half of Northeast Texas and Northern Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread trees and powerlines were downed throughout the entire parish.  Some trees were on homes and vehicles across the parish as well.  Power outages were widespread.

A strong cold front moved across southern Mississippi, southeast Louisiana and the adjacent coastal waters during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. Several reports of severe weather were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Vinson Road off Deer Run, as well as at the intersection of Louisiana Highways 41 and 36.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms continued to develop along a southeastward moving cold front during the predawn hours on the 23rd across Northwest Louisiana.  Weak disturbances aloft and the front itself provided the lift necessary for isolated strong storms, one of which produced dime sized hail across Caddo Parish.

Another upper level disturbance moved across the Middle Red River Valley of Southeast Oklahoma, the northern half of Northeast Texas and Southwest Arkansas as well as portions of North Central Louisiana during the afternoon and evening hours of February 7th. This system overran an cold airmass at the surface and as a result, some light freezing rain mixed with sleet was reported across Union and Ouachita Parishes in North Central Louisiana.  Ice amounts were near one tenth of an inch which resulted in a few slick spots across elevated bridges and overpasses.

Another upper level disturbance moved across the Middle Red River Valley of Southeast Oklahoma, the northern half of Northeast Texas and Southwest Arkansas as well as portions of North Central Louisiana during the afternoon and evening hours of February 7th. This system overran an cold airmass at the surface and as a result, some light freezing rain mixed with sleet was reported across Union and Ouachita Parishes in North Central Louisiana.  Ice amounts were near one tenth of an inch which resulted in a few slick spots across elevated bridges and overpasses.

A very cold...yet shallow airmass moved into the region earlier on February 9th.  Warm moist air overran the shallow cold airmass closer to the surface and with the aid of isentropic lift, some light freezing rain was reported across portions of Northwest Louisiana.  Freezing rain accumulations were mainly near or less than a tenth of an inch of precipitation.  The impacts were some slick spots reported on area bridges and overpasses.
Heavy rain in slow moving thunderstorms produced flooding in Leesville and along Bayou Anacoco.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of rain during a slow moving thunderstorm produced flooding over portions of Vernon Parish during the evening of the 12th. Multiple streets were flooded and closed in Leesville and Anacoco during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in slow moving thunderstorms produced flooding in Leesville and along Bayou Anacoco.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell during the evening of the 12th in portions of Vernon Parish and drained into Bayou Anacoco. Moderate flooding was noted along the bayou with some roadways being flooded.

A very unstable airmass developed south of the boundary with CAPE values approaching 4000 J/KG and Lifted Indices near -10. While low level shear was weak in the warm sector, deep layer shear was in excess of 50kts and with the help of a strong upper level trough moving out of Central Texas, severe thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon and evening hours of March 28th. Strong lapse rates initially resulted in hail producing thunderstorms, some of which took on the appearance of supercell thunderstorms. Later in the evening, a squall line developed with widespread wind damage noted across the southern half of Northeast Texas and Northern Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread trees and powerlines downed...mainly across the southern half of De Soto parish.  Power outages were widespread as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold...yet shallow airmass moved into the region earlier on February 9th.  Warm moist air overran the shallow cold airmass closer to the surface and with the aid of isentropic lift, some light freezing rain was reported across portions of Northwest Louisiana.  Freezing rain accumulations were mainly near or less than a tenth of an inch of precipitation.  The impacts were some slick spots reported on area bridges and overpasses.

With a very cold atmosphere already in place, an upper level storm system moved out of the Texas Hill Country and into Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas and Northwest Louisiana during the afternoon hours of February 11th. Precipitation became widespread across the region late in the afternoon and especially during the overnight hours of February 11th and the 12th. During the onset of precipitation, a mixture of rain and sleet was the predominant precipitation type with some sleet accumulations across West Central Louisiana. During the evening and overnight hours of the event, the transition turned to predominantly freezing rain with accumulations across West Central Louisiana near one tenth of an inch. Impacts included some slick spots on elevated bridges and overpasses, as well as some isolated power outages from downed limbs on powerlines.
A strong low pressure system produced numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across southeast Louisiana during the evening hours of the 6th and early morning hours of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported near Old Hammond Highway and Jefferson Highway in Baton Rouge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across southeast Louisiana during the evening hours of the 6th and early morning hours of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 20 trees were snapped and uprooted along East Domiano Lane. The NWS storm survey determined that the damage was from straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bitterly cold airmass descended on southern Mississippi and southeast Louisiana during the night of the 26th and during the day on the 27th. A strong upper level jet disturbance moved across the area on the 28th, producing a large area of sleet and freezing rain. Temperatures remained at or below the freezing mark during much or all of the precipitation event on the 28th, even south of Lake Pontchartrain in the New Orleans metropolitan area. Travel was significantly impacted on bridges, overpasses and other elevated roadways. Portions of every Interstate in southeast Louisiana, plus the Lake Pontchartrain Causeway were closed at times on the 28th into the morning of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was beginning to accumulate in Independence. Vehicles were stopping on the side of the road to clear windshields and free wipers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into South Louisiana during the morning of the 4th spawning severe storms and a couple of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado snapped or uprooted 10 to 15 trees on Kingery, Mattie Reeves, and Topsy Roads. Some of these trees blocked roads and driveways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the area during the 20th. Thunderstorms occurred during the evening with a tornado occurring near Ville Platte. Additional storms occurred a couple days later as the front returned north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado occurred near the Vidrine community along highway 10. The tornado was on the ground for around 2.7 miles and destroyed several garages and barns. It also damaged several homes by removing siding and shingles. One home had a tree fall on it trapping residents inside. The local fire department rescued them and they did not have any injuries. The tornado also downed several power poles along highway 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the area during the 20th. Thunderstorms occurred during the evening with a tornado occurring near Ville Platte. Additional storms occurred a couple days later as the front returned north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across portions of Lafayette producing gusty winds. A wooden Mardi Gras float was rolled and destroyed in the staging area near Cajun Field of UL of Lafayette. Other floats that were steel framed were undamaged. Small pea to dime sized hail was also reported with this storm.

A cold front moved through southern Mississippi and southeast Louisiana on January 23, bringing sub-freezing temperatures to much of the area. An upper level low pressure moved through this cold air during the night of the 23rd and morning of the 24th. A combination of freezing rain, sleet and snow fell across the area. Although amounts of the freezing and frozen precipitation generally remained below one quarter inch liquid equivalent, the precipitation produced very hazardous road conditions. Especially affected were bridges, overpasses and other elevated roadways. Travel was especially impacted in the Baton Rouge area with much of the interstate system shut down for much of the day on the 24th into the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of light rain and light freezing rain occurred across the metro area. Some elevated bridges had ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough moved out of the Texas Hill Country...towards the region during the late morning, afternoon and evening hours of May 9th. Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed in the unstable airmass with large hail and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts the primary result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in the North Highland area of Shreveport.  Some of these trees fell on homes. There were no reports of injuries.  This damage was a result of a rear flank downdraft that came out of a supercell thunderstorm that skirted the northern most portions of Shreveport.

Large hail and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts were the primary effects from the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officer estimated 60 mph thunderstorm wind gusts which downed trees across the Heflin community along with dime size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong return of moisture from the south, warm February temperatures and an unstable airmass were the ingredients needed for strong to severe thunderstorms to move across Northwest Louisiana during the afternoon hours of February 20th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sheds were blown down approximately 4 miles west of Barksdale AFB.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary pulled up stationary across portions of Southwest Arkansas into Southeast Oklahoma during the daytime hours. A very unstable airmass developed south of the boundary with CAPE values approaching 4000 J/KG and Lifted Indices near -10. While low level shear was weak in the warm sector, deep layer shear was in excess of 50kts and with the help of a strong upper level trough moving out of Central Texas, severe thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon and evening hours of March 28th. Strong lapse rates initially resulted in hail producing thunderstorms, some of which took on the appearance of supercell thunderstorms. Later in the evening, a squall line developed with widespread wind damage noted across the southern half of Northeast Texas and Northern Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported east of Dubach, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching southeast Louisiana from the west aided in the development of strong thunderstorms across the area. Several reports of hail were received.
A cold front approaching southeast Louisiana from the west aided in the development of strong thunderstorms across the area. Several reports of hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to dime size hail was reported in Gray.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across southeast Louisiana during the evening hours of the 6th and early morning hours of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roofing was blown off of a shed due to strong thunderstorm winds east of Wilmer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across southern Mississippi and southeast Louisiana during the evening of the 20th and the early morning of the 21st. A squall line developed in advance of the front with a few storms in the line becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 mph was reported by a television station at Benson Towers near the Superdome.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across southern Mississippi and southeast Louisiana during the evening of the 20th and the early morning of the 21st. A squall line developed in advance of the front with a few storms in the line becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report of a 65 mph wind gust at the St. Bernard Government Center in Chalmette. The event time was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening of Sunday, June 8th across Southwest Arkansas and Northern Louisiana, along and just ahead of a weak surface front and accompanying upper level disturbance. Some of these storms became severe, producing large hail, damaging winds, and even several reports of funnel clouds. The storms diminished during the mid evening hours as they moved into a more stabilized air mass from earlier showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported east of Farmerville, Louisiana on Willie Brantley Road off Hwy. 2E.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass allowed for the development of afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms. Some of these storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to golf ball sized hail was reported along Hangout Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early afternoon hours of July 23rd, a complex of showers and thunderstorms moved southeast into Eastern Oklahoma and Northwest Arkansas, after originally developing early that morning across portions of Nebraska and central Kansas. This complex encountered a very unstable air mass near a weak surface trough over Northern and Eastern Oklahoma and Northern Arkansas as it continued southeastward. At the same time, other scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over Central and Eastern Arkansas near this surface trough, while also aided by a weak upper level disturbance sinking south across Arkansas and the Mid-Mississippi Valley. The Eastern Oklahoma storm complex absorbed the growing storm complex over Central Arkansas, resulting in a large mesoscale convective system (MCS) intensifying and surging south into Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas during the mid and late afternoon hours. Widespread wind damage was reported across much of Central and Southern Arkansas as well as Southeast Oklahoma, with a large cold pool of air surging south into Northeast Texas and North Louisiana by late afternoon and early evening. Wind gusts ranged from 50-70 mph with this MCS as it surged south across much of the Ark-La-Tex, resulting in numerous downed trees, power lines, and structural damage. This storm complex weakened during the early evening hours, although the resultant cold pool still produced 40-45 mph wind gusts which produced minor tree damage over portions of Deep East Texas that did not see showers or thunderstorms. Over 500,000 AEP SWEPCO customers were without power after the storms blew through, with many thousands of other customers with other utility companies also without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed a tree that fell on a home north of Minden, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another complex of showers and thunderstorms developed during the predawn hours of Tuesday, June 10th which resulted in a large area of heavy rain falling over the Sam Rayburn and lower Toledo Bend country of Deep East Texas and the southern sections of North Central Louisiana. These storms redeveloped and moved over the same areas early Tuesday morning, with widespread 3-6+ inches of rain falling over a 2-3 hour period. Thus, flash flooding was observed over southern Sabine and western Natchitoches Parishes, with at least a couple of roads being washed away in flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Plainview Road was washed out where it intersects with a creek that runs east southeast from Hodges Gardens State Park just north of the Vernon Parish line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the late morning across Deep East Texas and Central Louisiana near an upper level disturbance, and advanced quickly north across extreme Eastern Texas, North Louisiana, and Southwest Arkansas during the mid and late afternoon hours. These storms consistently produced wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph, which resulted in downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and powerlines were downed all across the parish.  Trees were blocking many primary and secondary roadways.  Shingles were blown from the roof of the new sheriffs office on Holder Road in Farmerville, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The ridge broke down across the region and deep moisture was pumped north out of the gulf ahead of a trough of low pressure. This resulted in multiple days of heavy rain, severe weather, and even tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in rural Jefferson Davis Parish near Thornwell. It was videotaped by several people. The tornado touched down on Goose Island Road where it blew trees down. When it crossed Highway 14 it damaged a small barn and several trees. The tornado paralleled Highway 99 as it moved north remaining over mostly crawfish and rice fields. A couple of power lines were bent and one was snapped. A larger barn was destroyed along Highway 99 and several more trees snapped. The tornado dissipated along west Niblett Road where it blew a tree across a driveway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into the region causing numerous thunderstorms that produced flash flooding across Acadiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A modest amount of rain fell across the area during the 17th into the early morning of the 18th with the New Iberia area receiving between 1 and 3 inches. Heavy rain began during the morning of the 18th as a slow moving cold front moved in producing an additional 3 to 6 inches. The rain began to flood roadways by mid morning forcing their closure. By mid day to early afternoon the rain began to decrease in intensity, but not before many roads around New Iberia had been flooded including Highway 90.

This caused numerous roadways to be flooded from Carencro to the City of Lafayette while the rain was ongoing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms formed along a residual outflow boundary in Northern Louisiana and West Central Mississippi and in an upper level deformation zone induced axis of confluence farther to the south. The combination of strong instability and a rich moisture supply in the presence of weak but deep shear allowed thunderstorms to form over North Central Louisiana. These storms produced a series of cold air outflow boundaries that aided in additional storms forming as they intersected downstream to the south and southwest.  Wind damage and large hail was reported with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in the Whitehall community southesat of Jena, Louisiana on Hwy. 84.
